text: The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the left ureter before isolating the superior hemorrhoidal artery or inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The vessel is thinned out and clipped before division, while continuing the dissection laterally.
The surgeon visually inspects the left ureter to ensure its position and integrity during the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to elevate the rectus sigmoid and rectum from the pelvis by releasing adhesions on the left sidewall, joining the previously medially dissected plane.
The surgeon works down to the area where the bowel is adherent to the left pelvic sidewall and the left adnexa, carefully dissecting to release these adhesions.

The surgeon performs a full posterior dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, having achieved some release on the left side. The dissection involves mobilizing the rectum posteriorly, with plans for right lateral and anterior mobilization while leaving the left lateral side untouched.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to carefully dissect and release the adherent tissue from the left adnexa, employing a finger fracture technique to separate it from the pelvic sidewall. The dissection is performed with caution to avoid the ureter, which is not catheterized in this case.

The surgeon identifies the location for the distal margin and elevates the rectum out of the pelvis, preparing to complete the right lateral and anterior dissection. The deep cul-de-sac is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places a suture to suspend the uterus, facilitating the dissection in a three-port laparoscopic setup, including an umbilical Hassan port for the camera.

The surgeon utilizes a ten millimeter camera inserted through a twelve millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant and a five millimeter trocar in the right mid quadrant for visualization during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon re-enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane to facilitate the anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, preparing for potential mesh rectopexy.
The surgeon performs digital palpation to confirm the depth of the dissection, then proceeds to complete the posterior dissection in the pelvic region.

The surgeon follows the curve of the sacrum to the coccyx, reaching as far down as possible, while clearing the recto-sigmoid mesentery. The mesentery is put on stretch to enhance visibility during the dissection.
The surgeon exteriorizes the bowel through a small Pfannenstiel incision and places the anvil for a stapled anastomosis.

The surgeon takes the proximal mesentery and divides it distally after checking the blood supply. The bowel is exteriorized, and the anvil is placed for an EEA anastomosis, connecting the side of the proximal colon to the end of the rectum.
The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to assess blood supply and places a clip at the area of demarcation. The bowel is then exteriorized under white light, and a distal staple is applied.

The surgeon performs a rectopexy using 2-0 silk sutures on an SH needle, taking a bite of the periosteum and rectal mesentery without including the bowel wall. An assistant helps retract the bowel over the promontory using the fan and steel port, facilitating the suturing process.

The surgeon places a second suture during the rectopexy, ensuring the periosteum is exposed to avoid vascular tissue. Bipolar energy is used to cauterize visible blood vessels before suturing. The second stitch is placed lower on the rectum to aid in its elevation, with the surgeon checking the integrity of the suture after it snaps.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, observing no leakage, indicating a successful seal.
